1: The tôlĕḏôt formula — "This is the account of Shem, Ham and Japheth" — opens the Table of Nations, the most comprehensive ancient ethnographic document from the ANE. No other ancient text attempts to trace all known peoples to a single family origin with the systematic comprehensiveness of Gen 10. The Table lists 70 peoples (a number that likely represents the totality of known nations — cf. the 70 elders of Israel in Exodus 24:1; the 70 nations in rabbinic tradition). The phrase "who themselves had sons after the flood" anchors the Table in the post-flood covenant context: every people listed is a beneficiary of the Noahic covenant (Genesis 9:10-16).
2: The sons of Japheth are predominantly the Indo-European peoples of the north and west: Gomer (the Cimmerians, known in Assyrian records as Gimirrai; Ezekiel 38:6), Magog (applied in Ezek 38-39 to a northern enemy power; traditionally identified with Scythians), Madai (the Medes — major ANE power that co-conquered Babylon with Cyrus in 539 BCE), Javan (the Ionians/Greeks — Iavan is the Hebrew form of Iōnia; Daniel 8:21), Tubal (the Tabali of Asia Minor; Ezekiel 27:13), Meshek (the Mushki of Asia Minor, also in Ezekiel 27:13; Genesis 38:2), Tiras (uncertain — possibly Etruscans or Thracians). The Japhethic line encompasses the peoples of Asia Minor, the Aegean, the Iranian plateau, and the northern steppes.
3: Sons of Gomer: Ashkenaz (the Ashkuz of Assyrian records — a Scythian people from the Black Sea region; Jeremiah 51:27; in later Jewish usage, Ashkenaz refers to Germany, hence Ashkenazi Jews), Riphath (uncertain location — possibly the Riphaean mountains of ancient geography), Togarmah (Til-Garimmu in Assyrian sources — a kingdom in eastern Asia Minor; Ezekiel 27:14 mentions Togarmah trading horses and mules with Tyre; Ezekiel 38:6 places it in "the far north").
4: Sons of Javan (Greeks): Elishah (possibly Cyprus — the island of Alashiya was known to the Hittites as a copper source; Ezekiel 27:7), Tarshish (a distant western trading partner associated with silver, tin, and iron — Tartessos in Spain or Sardinia are the most plausible identifications; Jonah 1:3's Tarshish represents the far end of the known world), Kittites (Kition on Cyprus — later applied broadly to Greeks and then Romans in Daniel 11:30), Rodanites (Rhodes — some manuscripts read Dodanites/Dodanim; the MKT follows the MT Rodanites). The sons of Javan represent the Greek-speaking maritime world of the eastern Mediterranean.
5: "From these the maritime peoples spread out into their territories by their clans within their nations, each with its own language" — the closing formula introduces a narrative complexity: Genesis 10 describes peoples with distinct languages, yet Genesis 11 begins with "the whole world had one language." The relationship between these chapters is not strictly chronological: Genesis 10 presents the result (diverse nations), Genesis 11 explains the mechanism (Babel causing the diversity), and Genesis 12 narrows to the covenant-line (Abram). The literary order is thematic — global scope (Genesis 10) → the mechanism of dispersal (Genesis 11) → the covenant response to human rebellion (Genesis 12).
6: The sons of Ham are the northeastern African and southwestern Asian peoples: Cush (the region of Nubia/Ethiopia/Sudan — south of Egypt along the Nile; a major civilization and trading partner of Egypt), Egypt (Miṣrayim, the standard Hebrew name for Egypt — the dual form may indicate Upper and Lower Egypt), Put (Libya or broader North Africa — Nahum 3:9; Ezekiel 27:10), Canaan (the Canaanite peoples of the promised land, whose land was cursed in Genesis 9:25). The Hamitic genealogy establishes the ethnic relationships between Africa, the Arabian peninsula, and the Canaanite cities that Israel will encounter.
7: Sons of Cush: Seba (possibly a Nubian people — Psalms 72:10 names Seba alongside Sheba), Havilah (possibly in Arabia — cf. Genesis 2:11 where Havilah is associated with gold), Sabtah, Raamah, Sabteka (all likely Arabian trading peoples). Sons of Raamah: Sheba (the Sabaean kingdom of southern Arabia/Ethiopia — the Queen of Sheba who visited Solomon came from this wealthy trade empire, 1 Kings 10:1-13; Psalms 72:10) and Dedan (an oasis trading center in northwest Arabia — a hub on the incense route; Jeremiah 25:23; Ezekiel 27:15,20). The clustering of these Arabian trading peoples in the Cushite line reflects their historical role as intermediaries between Africa and the Fertile Crescent.
8: Nimrod is the Table's most narratively developed figure — the only person whose character is described rather than merely listed. "Cush was the father of Nimrod, who became a mighty warrior (gibbôr) on the earth" — the word gibbôr connects Nimrod to the pre-flood heroic culture (Genesis 6:4: the Nephilim are called gibbôrîm). Nimrod is the post-flood embodiment of the pre-flood hero tradition: a mighty man who built the first empire. The name Nimrod has been connected with Naram-Sin (the Akkadian king who deified himself) and with the epic hero Gilgamesh (who is also a great hunter and city-builder in the Gilgamesh epic). Whether these are direct identifications or typological parallels, Nimrod represents the Hamite imperial ambition that will manifest at Babel.
9: "A mighty hunter (gibbôr ṣayid) before the LORD (lipnê YHWH)" — the phrase lipnê YHWH (before/in the face of the LORD) is ambiguous: it may function as a superlative ("the greatest hunter in YHWH's assessment") or may carry the nuance of defiant boldness in YHWH's presence — hunting before/against YHWH as a rival. The same phrase is used in positive contexts (Noah walked before God, Genesis 6:9) and neutral superlative contexts (Numbers 3:4), so the ambiguity is genuine. The proverb "like Nimrod, a mighty hunter before the LORD" was current in Israelite tradition — Nimrod was already a legendary figure when Genesis was written, just as Gilgamesh was legendary in Mesopotamia. The naming of a proverb about him implies widespread cultural recognition.
10: "The first centers of his kingdom were Babylon, Uruk, Akkad and Kalneh, in Shinar" — these are historically attested Mesopotamian cities: Babylon (the great city of Babylonia — later Nebuchadnezzar's capital; the city that will conquer Jerusalem in 587 BCE), Uruk (ancient Erech — one of the oldest cities in the world, ca. 4000 BCE; home of the historical Gilgamesh tradition), Akkad (capital of the Akkadian Empire under Sargon the Great, ca. 2334-2279 BCE — the first truly imperial state in the ANE), Kalneh (uncertain — possibly Nippur or another Babylonian city). "Shinar" is the biblical name for the Babylonian plain — the same word used in 11:2 for the Babel location. Nimrod is credited with founding the first historical empire: the Mesopotamian city-state system.
11: "From that land he went to Assyria, where he built Nineveh, Rehoboth Ir, Calah" — the Assyrian cities are equally historical: Nineveh (capital of the Neo-Assyrian Empire at its height; Jonah's destination; destroyed in 612 BCE by the Babylonians and Medes), Calah (Kalhu/Nimrud in modern Iraq — the administrative capital of the Assyrian Empire under Ashurnasirpal II and Shalmaneser III; excavated in the 19th century revealing extensive palace complexes and Assyrian ivories). Rehoboth Ir (literally "city plazas/wide streets") is uncertain — possibly a district name rather than a separate city. Nimrod as founder of both Babylon and Nineveh establishes him as the progenitor of the two great empires that dominated Israel's history (Assyria destroyed northern Israel in 722 BCE; Babylon destroyed Jerusalem in 587 BCE).
12: "Resen, which is between Nineveh and Calah — which is the great city" — Resen's location between Nineveh and Calah (approximately 30 kilometers apart in the Tigris River valley) has not been definitively identified archaeologically. "Which is the great city" may refer to Resen, Nineveh, or Calah — the reference is ambiguous. The Assyrian capital region (including Nineveh, Calah, Khorsabad, and Assur) formed a metropolitan complex sometimes called the "Assyrian triangle." The narrative's attention to the great cities of the Nimrodic empire is a theological geography: these will be the adversaries of YHWH's covenant people throughout the biblical narrative.
13: "Egypt was the father of the Ludites, Anamites, Lehabites, Naphtuhites" — these are Egyptian-derived peoples, most of whose exact identifications are uncertain: Ludites (possibly the Lydians of western Asia Minor, though this conflicts with the Shemite identification of Lud in v22), Anamites (unknown), Lehabites (possibly related to Libya — Lubim), Naphtuhites (possibly the people of northern Egypt — Npt in Egyptian texts). The Egyptian genealogy establishes the broad cultural sphere of Egyptian influence in northeastern Africa and the eastern Mediterranean coast.
14: "Pathrusites, Kasluhites (from whom the Philistines came) and Caphtorites" — Pathrusites are the people of Pathros (Upper Egypt — the Nile valley south of Memphis; Isaiah 11:11; Jeremiah 44:1). Kasluhites and Caphtorites are both associated with the Philistines — Amos 9:7 identifies the Philistines as coming from Caphtor (Crete or the Aegean islands), consistent with the archaeological evidence that the Philistines were part of the "Sea Peoples" migration from the Aegean world ca. 1200 BCE. The Philistines' placement in the Hamitic genealogy through Egypt establishes the theological context for the later Philistine-Israel conflict: the Philistines are descended from Ham, whose line includes those under the curse of Canaan (Genesis 9:25).
15: "Canaan was the father of Sidon his firstborn, and of the Hittites" — Sidon (the great Phoenician city on the Lebanese coast — a major Mediterranean trading power; the firstborn of Canaan indicates its prominence) and the Hittites (the Neo-Hittite city-states of Syria-Palestine, who were the remnants of the great Hittite Empire of Anatolia — 1600-1178 BCE). The inclusion of the Hittites in the Canaanite line explains the biblical usage of "Hittites" as Canaanite peoples (cf. Genesis 23:3-20: Abraham buying the cave of Machpelah from the "Hittites" who lived in Hebron, who are local Canaanites, not the imperial Hittites of Anatolia). The genealogy is the Table of Nations' explanation for who the Israelites will encounter when they enter the promised land.
16: Jebusites (the pre-Israelite inhabitants of Jerusalem — the city David captures in 2 Samuel 5:6-8 and renames the City of David; Araunah the Jebusite's threshing floor becomes the temple mount in 2 Samuel 24:18-25), Amorites (a broad term covering various Syro-Canaanite peoples — Hammurabi of Babylon was Amorite; the five Amorite kings of Joshua 10:5 are southern Canaanite rulers; in Amos 2:9-10 the Amorites represent the entire pre-Israelite population of Canaan), Girgashites (a Canaanite people listed in the conquest accounts — Deuteronomy 7:1; Joshua 3:10 — but otherwise unknown in extra-biblical sources).
17: Hivites (associated with Shechem in Gen 34 and Gibeon in Josh 9; a Canaanite people whose exact identity is uncertain — some MSS and the LXX confuse Hivites with Horites/Hurrians), Arkites (inhabitants of Arka on the Lebanese coast — a city known from Egyptian texts), Sinites (uncertain — possibly on the Lebanese coast). These less prominent Canaanite groups are included in the Table's comprehensive scope: the promise of the land to Abraham's descendants encompasses the full range of its current inhabitants, not just the major powers.
18: Arvadites (Arvad/Aradus — an island city off the Syrian coast; a significant Phoenician trading center; Ezekiel 27:8,11), Zemarites (Sumur/Simyra — a Syrian coastal city known from the Amarna letters), Hamathites (Hamath — a major city on the Orontes River in Syria, the northernmost boundary of the Davidic kingdom, 2 Samuel 8:9; 1 Kings 8:65). "Later the Canaanite clans scattered" — the dispersion of the Canaanite peoples into their territories is the post-flood fulfillment of the Table's geographic claims: these peoples are where they are because of the genealogical relationships traced here.
19: "The borders of Canaan reached from Sidon toward Gerar as far as Gaza, and then toward Sodom, Gomorrah, Admah and Zeboyim, as far as Lasha" — the territorial definition of Canaan maps the precise region that YHWH will give to Abraham's descendants. Sidon (north) to Gaza (southwest coast) to Sodom/Gomorrah (south, in the Dead Sea region) defines the territory. The inclusion of Sodom and Gomorrah in Canaan's territorial borders is deliberate: the cities that will be destroyed in Genesis 19 are within the promised land. The Table of Nations thus maps not only the ethnic geography of the ANE but the specific territory of the Abrahamic covenant promise.
20: The closing formula for Ham's genealogy — "by their clans (lĕmišpĕḥōtām) and languages (liĕšōnōtām), in their territories (bĕʾarṣōtām) and nations (bĕgôyēhem)" — uses four categories that structure the Table of Nations as a whole. The fourfold taxonomy (clan + language + territory + nation) describes human social organization in its full complexity: the Table is not merely a genealogy but an ethnography, mapping the intersection of kinship, language, geography, and political identity. This is the most developed sociological taxonomy in any ancient Near Eastern text before the Assyrian annals.
21: "Shem was the ancestor of all the sons of Eber (bĕnê ʿēḇer)" — the designation of Shem as the ancestor of the sons of Eber is the Table's most significant theological marker: ʿēḇer (Eber) is the eponymous ancestor of the Hebrews (ʿiḇrî — Hebrew). The term "Hebrew" derives from this genealogical line. The Table of Nations identifies the covenant-seed line within the universal human family: out of all 70 nations, one line within Shem's genealogy (Shem → Arphaxad → Shelah → Eber → Peleg → Reu → Serug → Nahor → Terah → Abram) carries the covenant promise forward. The Table contextualizes this narrowing: the covenant with Abram is not tribal favoritism but the selection of one thread from the full tapestry of human diversity.
22: The sons of Shem: Elam (the Elamite kingdom east of Mesopotamia — a major ANE power; Elam captures Lot in Genesis 14:1-12; Isaiah 11:11 includes Elamites in the diaspora), Asshur (Assyria — note the complexity: Asshur appears here as a Shemite, yet in vv11-12 Nimrod the Hamite builds Nineveh; the table reflects the layered ethnic history of Assyria as both Semitic in language and mixed in population), Arphaxad (the key line to Abram — traced in Genesis 11's genealogy), Lud (possibly the Lydians of western Asia Minor, though this conflicts with Ludites being Hamitic in v13 — the identification is debated), Aram (the Aramean people of Syria — Israel's close relatives, with whom they share significant cultural and linguistic affinity; Aramaic became the diplomatic language of the ANE and later the common language of Palestine in Jesus's day).
23: Sons of Aram: Uz (the land where Job lived — Job 1:1 — placing Job within the Aramean/Shemite cultural sphere; Jeremiah 25:20 lists Uz alongside the Philistines and Edom), Hul (uncertain — possibly a region east of the Jordan), Gether (uncertain), Meshek (in the MT — possibly distinct from the Japhethite Meshek of v2, though some read Mash/Massa here; the LXX reads Mash). The sons of Aram establish the ethnic geography of Syria and the Transjordanian regions — peoples with whom Israel had both kinship ties (Jacob's mother Rebekah was from Aram, Genesis 25:20) and conflict.
24: Arphaxad → Shelah → Eber — the three-generation descent to Eber (the eponymous ancestor of the Hebrews) traces the covenant-line within the Shemite genealogy. The progression narrows rapidly: from all of Shem's descendants (five sons, v22) to one son (Arphaxad) to one grandson (Shelah) to one great-grandson (Eber) who will give his name to the people of God. The genealogy demonstrates the principle of selective narrowing that governs the entire biblical narrative: from all humanity (Genesis 10) to one man (Genesis 12), the covenant line is progressively specified.
25: "Two sons were born to Eber: One was named Peleg, because in his time the earth was divided (nipleḡāh)" — the name Peleg (pālag = to divide/split) is explained by the division of the earth. The natural referent for "division of the earth" is the Babel dispersion (Genesis 11) — the Hebrew wordplay (Peleg / niplĕgāh) connects the genealogical name to the historical event. The notation "in his time" anchors the Babel event chronologically within the Shemite genealogy. The divided earth of Peleg's time is the world that Abram will be called out of in Genesis 12 — the world of many languages and many nations, into which the covenant blessing will flow (Genesis 12:3).
26: Joktan's line (vv26-29) represents the tribes of the Arabian peninsula — 13 sons covering the geography of ancient Arabia from Yemen to the Persian Gulf. Almodad (unknown), Sheleph (unknown), Hazarmaveth (Haḑramawt — the well-known region of southern Arabia in modern Yemen; one of the most distinctive geographical identifications in the Table, verifiable from ancient South Arabian inscriptions), Jerah (possibly a lunar-calendar people — yāreḥ = moon). The Arabian genealogy establishes the ethnic background of the peoples living in the territories between Israel and Egypt — significant for the later caravans (Ishmaelites, Midianites, Sabeans) that appear in the Joseph and Job narratives.
27: Hadoram, Uzal (Ṣanʿāʾ, the capital of modern Yemen — medieval Arabic sources identify Uzal with Sana'a, the ancient capital of Arabia Felix), Diklah (possibly the palm-tree region of Arabia — dĕqel = palm tree in Aramaic). These Arabian tribal ancestors establish the genealogical context for the "sons of the East" (bĕnê qedem) who appear throughout the OT — the semi-nomadic trading peoples of the Syrian-Arabian desert whose caravans connected Egypt with Mesopotamia.
28: Obal (unknown location), Abimael (unknown — the ab-il element suggests a theophoric name meaning "God is my father," unusual in an ethnic genealogy), Sheba (the Sabaean kingdom — appearing here in the Joktanite line and also in v7 in the Cushite line, reflecting the mixed ethnic heritage of the ancient South Arabian kingdoms; the Queen of Sheba who visits Solomon represents this wealthy spice-and-gold trading civilization, 1 Kings 10:1-13).
29: Ophir (the famous gold-producing region associated with Solomon's naval expeditions — 1 Kings 9:28; Genesis 10:11; Job 22:24; Psalms 45:9; the location of Ophir is debated: India, Arabia, and eastern Africa have all been proposed), Havilah (appearing also in v7 in the Cushite line — indicating the overlapping territorial and ethnic claims of Arabian geography), Jobab (possibly connected with Job's name — ʾiyyôḇ — placing the patriarch Job within the Joktanite Arabian sphere consistent with Job 1:1's identification of Uz as his homeland). The 13 sons of Joktan (unusual — other genealogies rarely list 13 descendants of one person) cover an unusually specific geographic area, suggesting the Table preserves specific ethnographic knowledge about southern Arabian tribes.
30: "The region where they lived stretched from Mesha toward Sephar, in the eastern hill country" — Mesha and Sephar are uncertain — possibly Massa in the Arabian peninsula (Genesis 25:14; 1 Chronicles 1:30) and Ẓafar in Yemen respectively. The "eastern hill country" (har haqqedem) is the highland region of Arabia. The geographic scope of the Joktanite territory (from northwest Arabia to Yemen) represents one of the most specific and geographically precise claims in the Table of Nations — the Arabian interior that was a major ancient trading zone.
31: The closing formula for Shem's genealogy mirrors the formula for Ham (v20) and anticipates the master closing formula of v32. The fourfold taxonomy (clans, languages, territories, nations) is applied consistently to each of Noah's three sons, giving the Table a formal symmetry that signals its character as a structured literary and ethnographic document, not merely a genealogical list. The consistent formula also emphasizes that the diversity of human cultures — languages, territories, nations — is a divinely ordered reality, not a defect requiring correction (until the disorder of Babel, Genesis 11).
32: The master closing formula — "these are the clans of Noah's sons, according to their lines of descent, within their nations. From these the nations spread out over the earth after the flood" — closes the Table with the universal scope statement that opened it (v1). Every people, every language, every territory ultimately traces to one surviving family. The Table of Nations is the biblical basis for the unity of humanity: the diversity of Genesis 10 is the diversity of one family. This universal human kinship is the foundation for the Abrahamic covenant's universal scope (Genesis 12:3: "all peoples on earth will be blessed through you") — the covenant blesses the family traced in this Table.
